Sin makes people cowards. Whoever faces difficulties in doing what is right is not discouraged.
National sins disrupt public peace.
If poor people get opportunities to take advantage of others, their extortion will be more severe than that of wealthier people.
Wicked people encourage each other in wicked ways.
If a person seeks the Lord, it’s a good sign that they understand much, and it's a good way to understand even more.
An honest, godly, poor man is better than a wicked, ungodly, rich man; has more comfort within himself, and is a greater blessing to the world.
Companions of riotous people not only grieve their parents but shame them.
What is gained dishonestly, even if it increases greatly, will not last long. This is how the poor are repaid, and God is glorified.
The sinner whose prayers provoke God’s anger is one who stubbornly refuses to obey God's commands.
The success of ungodly people is their own misery.
Rich people are so flattered that they think themselves superior to others.
There is glory in the land when righteous people have freedom.

It is foolish to indulge sin and excuse it. Whoever covers his sins will not have true peace. Whoever humbly confesses his sins with true repentance and faith will find mercy from God. The Son of God is our great atonement. Under a deep sense of our guilt and danger, we may claim salvation from that mercy which reigns through righteousness unto eternal life by Jesus Christ our Lord.
There is a fear that causes happiness. Faith and love will deliver from the fear of eternal misery; but we should always fear offending God and fear sinning against him.
A wicked ruler, whatever we may call him, this scripture calls a roaring lion and a ranging bear.
Oppressors lack understanding; they do not consider their own honor, ease, and safety.
The murderer will be haunted with terrors. No one will desire to save him from deserved punishment or pity him.
Righteousness gives people security in the worst times; but dishonest people are never safe.
Those who are diligent find a way to live comfortably.
The true way to be happy is to be holy and honest, not to increase an estate suddenly without regard to right or wrong.
Judgment is perverted when anything other than pure right is considered.
Whoever hurries to become rich never seriously thinks about how quickly God may take his wealth away and leave him in poverty.
Upon reflection, most will have a better opinion of a faithful reprover than of a soothing flatterer.
Here is the wickedness of those who think it no sin to rob their parents by wheedling them or threatening them, or by wasting what they have and running into debt.
Those make themselves easy when they live in continual dependence upon God and his grace and live by faith.
A fool trusts in his own strength, merit, and righteousness. And he trusts in his own heart, which is not only deceitful above all things but has often deceived him.
A selfish person will not look out for those who need compassion; instead, he turns away from those who call for his attention.

When power is put into the hands of wicked people, wise people decline public business. If the reader goes diligently over this and the other chapters, in many places where at first he may suppose there is least of Christ, still he will find what leads to him.
